content.text: Structured Logging and Diagnostics in Python

Every serious Python application — whether a data pipeline, API server, or AI model training script — needs a way to record what it's doing, catch errors early, and understand behavior in production. The
module in Python's standard library is the professional answer to that need. Unlike
statements, which are scattered and hard to control, the
module gives you a unified, configurable, hierarchical system for emitting diagnostic messages across your entire codebase.
module is Python's built-in framework for recording runtime events. It was inspired by Log4j (Java) and follows the same core design:
work together to route messages from anywhere in your code to any output destination — a terminal, a file, a remote server, or all three simultaneously.
It ships with Python and requires no installation. Import it and you have a production-grade logging infrastructure available immediately.
In the context of Generative AI and ML systems, logging is especially critical because:
Model training runs for hours or days — you need a record of what happened
Inference pipelines have latency requirements — you need timestamps and duration tracking
Prompt/response cycles need auditing for debugging and safety
Distributed training jobs run across many machines — centralized logs are essential
. When something goes wrong in production — or when a model produces unexpected output — you need answers.
gives you none of the tools needed to answer them systematically:
output to different destinations based on severity
You can't include structured metadata like timestamps, file names, or line numbers automatically
You can't silence third-party library output without modifying their code
module solves every one of these problems through a configurable, hierarchical system that separates the
The logging system is built from four interconnected components:
is the object your code directly interacts with. You retrieve one by name using
. Logger names form a dot-separated hierarchy —
— and child loggers inherit settings from their parents.
sits at the top of the hierarchy and is accessed via
with no name. Every named logger eventually propagates up to the root unless you stop it.
. The module defines five standard levels in ascending order:
Confirmation that things work as expected
Something unexpected, but the app continues
A serious problem — a function couldn't complete
A severe error that may stop the program
A logger only processes messages at or above its configured level. Setting a logger to

logger.debug("Batch %d loaded, shape: %s", batch_idx, str(batch.shape))
logger.info("Epoch %d complete. Loss: %.4f", epoch, loss)
logger.warning("Learning rate too high — may cause instability")
logger.error("Failed to load checkpoint: %s", checkpoint_path)
logger.critical("GPU out of memory. Terminating training.")
takes a log record and sends it somewhere. One logger can have multiple handlers — for example, one that writes everything to a file and another that prints only
File that rotates after a size limit
File that rotates on a time schedule
controls what a log message looks like. You can include timestamps, logger names, file names, line numbers, and the message itself.
Name of the function that logged

provides fine-grained control over which records a handler or logger actually processes. For example, you could filter to only pass records from a specific module, or only records where a custom attribute exists.
Architecture: How a Log Message Flows

Your Code
   │
   ▼
logger.info("message")
   │
   ▼
Logger checks: is INFO >= my effective level?
   │
   ├── No  → discard
   │
   └── Yes → create LogRecord object
                  │
                  ▼
            Apply Logger Filters
                  │
                  ├── Filtered out → discard
                  │
                  └── Passes → send to each Handler
                                    │
                                    ▼
                              Handler checks its own level
                                    │
                                    ▼
                              Apply Handler Filters
                                    │
                                    ▼
                              Formatter formats the record
                                    │
                                    ▼
                              Handler emits to destination
                                    │
                                    ▼
                              Propagate to parent Logger?
                              (if propagate=True, repeat up the tree)
This layered design means you can set coarse rules at the root and fine-grained rules in child loggers, without any module needing to know about the others.
How to Implement — Step by Step
sets up the root logger in one line:

import logging

logging.basicConfig(
    level=logging.DEBUG,
    format="%(asctime)s | %(levelname)s | %(message)s",
    datefmt="%H:%M:%S"
)

logging.info("Script started")
logging.debug("Debug detail here")
is a convenience function. It only has an effect the first time it is called — if the root logger already has handlers (as it does when using Jupyter or many frameworks), calling
again does nothing. This is a common source of confusion.
In every module of a larger project, get a module-level logger using

# myapp/trainer.py
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
# This creates a logger named "myapp.trainer"

def train_epoch(model, dataloader):
    logger.info("Starting epoch training with %d batches", len(dataloader))
    for i, batch in enumerate(dataloader):
        logger.debug("Processing batch %d", i)
        # ... training logic ...
    logger.info("Epoch complete")
is the canonical best practice because:
The logger name reflects the module structure exactly
You can control log levels per module from a central config
Third-party libraries do the same, so you can silence or amplify them selectively
For a real application, configure logging in one central place (usually

import logging
from logging.handlers import RotatingFileHandler

def setup_logging(log_level=logging.INFO, log_file="app.log"):
    root_logger = logging.getLogger()
    root_logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)  # Capture everything; handlers filter

    # Console handler — INFO and above
    console_handler = logging.StreamHandler()
    console_handler.setLevel(log_level)
    console_formatter = logging.Formatter(
        "%(asctime)s [%(levelname)s] %(name)s: %(message)s",
        datefmt="%H:%M:%S"
    )
    console_handler.setFormatter(console_formatter)

    # File handler — DEBUG and above, rotates at 10MB, keeps 5 backups
    file_handler = RotatingFileHandler(
        log_file, maxBytes=10 * 1024 * 1024, backupCount=5
    )
    file_handler.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
    file_formatter = logging.Formatter(
        "%(asctime)s | %(name)s | %(levelname)s | %(filename)s:%(lineno)d | %(message)s"
    )
    file_handler.setFormatter(file_formatter)

    root_logger.addHandler(console_handler)
    root_logger.addHandler(file_handler)
once at the entry point of your application and every module's
call will automatically use this configuration.
Step 4: Configuring via a Dictionary (Production Standard)
The most maintainable approach for production systems is
. It separates logging configuration from application logic entirely and can be loaded from a YAML or JSON file.
blocks to capture the full stack trace automatically:
, which captures and formats the current exception's traceback.

In Generative AI pipelines, plain text logs are often not enough. You need to query logs programmatically — find all requests where latency exceeded 500ms, or all prompts that triggered a safety filter. This requires
, where each log record is a JSON object.
To make these fields appear in the output, write a custom formatter:
When processing many requests concurrently, you want every log message from a request to carry a
wraps a logger and injects extra context into every call:

import logging

class RequestLogger(logging.LoggerAdapter):
    def process(self, msg, kwargs):
        kwargs.setdefault("extra", {})
        kwargs["extra"]["request_id"] = self.extra["request_id"]
        return msg, kwargs

base_logger = logging.getLogger("myapp.inference")

def handle_request(request_id, prompt):
    logger = RequestLogger(base_logger, {"request_id": request_id})
    logger.info("Received prompt, length=%d", len(prompt))
    # Every log call from here automatically includes request_id
Real-World Example: LLM Inference Service Logging
Consider a production service that wraps an LLM API and adds caching, rate limiting, and safety filtering. Here is how a complete logging setup looks:
Each JSON log line can then be ingested by a log aggregation system (Datadog, Grafana Loki, AWS CloudWatch) and queried by
, or any other field. This is the pattern used at scale by real AI serving systems.
for information that operators need in production, breaks the filtering system and forces noisy alerts. Match the level to the actual severity.
bypasses all handlers and filters. In a daemon, worker process, or containerized app,
output may be lost entirely. Commit fully to the logging module.
Library and framework authors should never call
or add handlers to the root logger. Doing so changes the logging configuration of every application that imports the library. Libraries should only call
and emit records — configuration is the application's responsibility.
4. String formatting inside the log call
This is a subtle but important performance mistake:

# Bad — string is formatted even if DEBUG is disabled
logger.debug("Batch shape: " + str(batch.shape))

# Bad — same problem with f-strings
logger.debug(f"Batch shape: {batch.shape}")

# Good — lazy formatting, only executed if the record is processed
logger.debug("Batch shape: %s", batch.shape)

-style formatting, the string is only assembled if the log record actually needs to be emitted. In a tight loop at DEBUG level (which is disabled in production), this can meaningfully impact performance.
when adding handlers directly to a named logger
and also have handlers on the root logger, messages from
will be processed by both, causing duplicate output. Set
on the named logger if you want it to handle its own output exclusively.
6. Not logging exceptions with stack traces
Logging raw prompts, API keys, user tokens, or PII creates a security and privacy liability. Always sanitize or truncate sensitive values before logging.
Configure once at startup, use everywhere.
Set the root logger level to
to silence noisy third-party libraries by default, and explicitly configure your own app's loggers to
in any application that runs continuously. Plain
Use JSON logging for any system that will be queried or aggregated.
Text logs are for humans; JSON logs are for both humans and machines.
in every log message for distributed systems, so you can correlate all events from a single user request across multiple services.
in high-throughput applications. This moves all I/O to a background thread and prevents logging from blocking your main event loop.
Never log inside hot loops at
. This keeps production logs clean and avoids I/O overhead.

In typical application code, logging overhead is negligible. However, in high-throughput systems (inference servers handling thousands of requests per second), logging I/O can become a bottleneck. The solution is

import logging
import logging.handlers
import queue

log_queue = queue.Queue(-1)  # No size limit

# All loggers send to the queue — non-blocking
queue_handler = logging.handlers.QueueHandler(log_queue)

# A background thread reads from the queue and writes to handlers
file_handler = logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler("app.log", maxBytes=10_000_000, backupCount=5)
listener = logging.handlers.QueueListener(log_queue, file_handler, respect_handler_level=True)
listener.start()

root = logging.getLogger()
root.addHandler(queue_handler)
root.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
call in your application just enqueues a record in memory (microsecond operation), and a separate thread handles the disk I/O. Under load, this can reduce logging latency by orders of magnitude.
is the correct approach, since file handles are not safely shared across processes.
Third-party structured logging library; adds rich context binding and pipeline-style processing
Third-party; simpler API than stdlib, built-in color, structured output
Drop-in JSON formatter for the standard logging module
Log aggregation and querying at scale
Managed log storage and alerting for cloud-hosted applications
Unified observability standard that integrates logs, metrics, and traces

For Generative AI systems specifically, OpenTelemetry is gaining adoption because it connects logs to distributed traces, making it possible to track a user request from the API gateway through the prompt router to the model and back — all in one unified view.
No levels, no routing, no format control, lost in production
Verbose configuration; plain text by default
Complex setup; overkill for single-service apps
For Generative AI work, the typical progression is:
with JSON output in production → OpenTelemetry for multi-service distributed systems.
how and where to log it
(configuration), making it suitable for everything from scripts to distributed AI systems.
in every module — never configure logging inside libraries, and never use
) are the primary tool for controlling verbosity across environments — development vs. production configurations differ only in their level settings.
blocks to capture full stack traces automatically.
For high-throughput AI inference services, switch to asynchronous logging with
Structured (JSON) logging is essential for any system where logs need to be searched, filtered, or aggregated by machine — which includes virtually every production AI application.
pattern is the production standard: define all logging configuration in a dictionary or YAML file and apply it once at application startup.
